original_hypothesis.title: ERC721Airdrop claim loop can be weaponized for gas-exhaustion DoS if merkle leaf allows large tokenIds arrays
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ERC721Airdrop.claim verifies a merkle leaf of (user, tokenIds) and then loops over tokenIds to transfer NFTs. If the merkle tree includes a leaf with a very large tokenIds array (by mistake or as part of a bulk grant), the claim may become uncallable due to block gas limits, effectively locking those NFTs in the vault for that recipient. Attackers cannot change the tree, but they can exploit operational mistakes by selecting a leaf with unbounded array size and demonstrating permanent non-claimability.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Airdrop datasets often include bulk allocations. Because this is a no-mempool chain and no MEV assumption, the practical risk is operational dataset construction leading to non-claimable leaves (funds locked), which real attackers can publicize and use to erode trust or to force emergency migration.
deployment_assumptions: Merkle tree includes leaves with large tokenIds arrays
Block gas limit is insufficient to complete the loop transfers
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: ERC721Airdrop.claim performs unbounded loop over tokenIds; MerkleClaimable leaf data includes tokenIds array with no on-chain size cap.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i; i < tokenIds.length; ++i) IERC721(token).safeTransferFrom(vault, user, tokenIds[i]);

preconditions: A merkle leaf exists with a tokenIds array length large enough to exceed gas limits
Vault holds/approves those NFTs for transfer
User attempts to claim via this single transaction path (no batching supported)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No batching or partial-claim mechanism; all NFTs in tokenIds must be transferred in one call.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Depends on dataset generation and merkle root content.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During claim window; permanent thereafter if unclaimable

operational_questions: Does the merkle generation pipeline cap tokenIds length per leaf?
Is there an emergency/manual distribution path for stuck leaves?
What is the chain's practical block gas limit during mainnet operation?
validation_requirements: Review merkle dataset sizes and maximum tokenIds length
Assess possibility of adding a batched/partial claim in a future upgrade

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a leaf with large tokenIds list (public dataset)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attempt claim; it consistently runs out of gas/reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Demonstrate NFTs are effectively locked unless admin intervenes

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Some recipients cannot claim NFTs; vault retains assets; reputational damage
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium—may require admin-side manual transfers or contract upgrade

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated failed claim txs due to out-of-gas
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unclaimed inventory remaining in vault post-claim window
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Specific users consistently failing to claim

validation_result.impact_summary: ERC721Airdrop.claim iterates over the entire tokenIds array in one transaction with no partial claim mechanism, potentially making leaves with large tokenIds arrays permanently unclaimable due to block gas limits.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ERC721Airdrop.claim function accepts an array of tokenIds, verifies them against a merkle leaf via _verifyClaim, and then loops through all tokenIds calling IERC721.safeTransferFrom for each one. The merkle proof verification hashes the entire (user, tokenIds) tuple, meaning partial claims are impossible — the user must claim ALL tokenIds in the leaf in a single transaction. There is no on-chain size cap on the tokenIds array. Each safeTransferFrom call costs at minimum ~50k-100k gas (ERC721 transfer + potential onERC721Received callback). If a merkle leaf contains, say, 500+ tokenIds, the transaction would require 25M-50M+ gas, potentially exceeding block gas limits. Since the isClaimed mapping uses the hash of the full (user, tokenIds) data, there is no way to split the claim into multiple transactions. The NFTs would remain permanently locked in the vault for that recipient unless the admin deploys a new merkle root or manually transfers the NFTs.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code at ERC721Airdrop.sol clearly shows (1) merkle verification of the complete (user, tokenIds) tuple, preventing partial claims, and (2) an unbounded loop performing external calls for each tokenId. MerkleClaimable._verifyClaim marks the hash as claimed atomically, so there is no retry or partial mechanism. This is a genuine design defect — the contract lacks any batching or partial claim functionality.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function verifies the full (user, tokenIds) tuple against the merkle tree, then loops through ALL tokenIds with external safeTransferFrom calls. No partial claim is possible since the merkle leaf hash covers the entire tokenIds array. A large tokenIds array would exceed block gas limits, making the claim permanently unexecutable.

The claim hash is computed over the entire data (including the full tokenIds array). Once claimed, it's marked in isClaimed. This atomic all-or-nothing design prevents partial claiming — the user cannot split a large tokenIds array into multiple transactions.

calling_sequence: 2. nonReentrant modifier checks and sets reentrancy lock
4. MerkleClaimable._verifyClaim hashes the FULL (user, tokenIds) tuple
5. Merkle proof is verified against merkleRoot
7. Loop: for each tokenId in tokenIds, IERC721.safeTransferFrom(vault, user, tokenId)
8. If gas runs out during the loop, entire transaction reverts including the isClaimed mark
9. User cannot retry with a subset of tokenIds because the merkle proof would fail
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability manifests when tokenIds.length is large enough that the cumulative gas cost of all safeTransferFrom calls exceeds the block gas limit. Since _verifyClaim uses keccak256(abi.encode('CLAIM_TAIKO_AIRDROP', abi.encode(user, tokenIds))) as the leaf hash, the exact tokenIds array is cryptographically bound to the merkle tree. The user cannot modify the array (e.g., claim a subset) without invalidating the proof. If the transaction consistently runs out of gas, the NFTs are effectively locked in the vault forever for that recipient.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Merkle tree includes leaves with large tokenIds arrays
Block gas limit is insufficient to complete the loop transfers
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No ERC721Airdrop deployment script is provided. The DeployERC20Airdrop.s.sol is present but does not address ERC721. The foundry.toml shows block_gas_limit = 80000000 (80M) for testing. The merkle tree construction is entirely off-chain and not visible in the provided scripts. No on-chain or off-chain size cap on tokenIds per leaf is evident.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid from a code defect perspective: if the merkle tree dataset generation pipeline does not cap tokenIds length per leaf, and any leaf exceeds the block gas limit threshold, those NFTs become permanently unclaimable through the contract's claim mechanism. Each IERC721.safeTransferFrom costs ~50-100k gas minimum, meaning ~800-1600 NFTs could be transferred in an 80M gas block. On Ethereum mainnet (30M limit), this drops to ~300-600. The defect exists regardless of whether any current merkle tree actually contains such large arrays — the contract provides no protection against this scenario.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM impact. If a merkle leaf with a sufficiently large tokenIds array exists, the associated NFTs are permanently locked in the vault for that recipient. Recovery requires admin intervention (manual transfer from vault or contract upgrade with new merkle root). The severity is bounded by the fact that this requires an operational mistake in merkle tree construction, but the code provides zero safeguards against it.
recommended_mitigations: Add a partial claim mechanism: allow claiming subsets of tokenIds with appropriate bookkeeping to prevent double-claims
Add an on-chain cap on tokenIds.length in the claim function (e.g., require(tokenIds.length <= MAX_CLAIM_BATCH))
Implement a claim-then-withdraw pattern similar to ERC20Airdrop2 where claims are recorded first and transfers happen in separate batched transactions
Enforce a maximum tokenIds length per leaf during off-chain merkle tree generation
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ERC721 token and mint 1000+ NFTs to a vault. 2. Deploy ERC721Airdrop with a merkle tree containing a leaf with 1000+ tokenIds for a single user. 3. Approve ERC721Airdrop as operator for the vault. 4. Attempt to call claim() with the full tokenIds array. 5. Observe that the transaction exceeds block gas limit and reverts. 6. Confirm no partial claim path exists.
uncertainties: The actual block gas limit of the target deployment chain is unknown from the provided materials
The merkle tree generation pipeline is entirely off-chain and not auditable here
Typical NFT airdrop sizes per recipient in Taiko's specific use case are unknown
additional_testing_needed: Gas benchmarking of safeTransferFrom for the specific ERC721 token to determine exact per-transfer cost
Review of the off-chain merkle tree generation code/scripts for tokenIds size caps
Determine the target chain's actual block gas limit at deployment time
Test with progressively larger tokenIds arrays to find the exact threshold
